understanding.goal_understanding: The goal of the game was to achieve a WIN state on each level, typically by maneuvering a pushable object (or otherwise interactable piece) onto one or more goal tiles marked with '+'. Once a level reported WIN, that level was solved; once a run reported WIN, the entire game was considered won. Each level had a limited number of steps, so the objective also included planning an efficient sequence of moves and clicks before the step counter reached zero.
understanding.mechanics_understanding: The world consisted of a grid with different tiles: empty floor, walls/obstacles, selectable pieces, pushable blocks, and special goal tiles '+'. You interacted indirectly through a selected piece. CLICK [x, y] changed which piece was selected; if a piece existed at the clicked coordinates and was selectable, the game moved the selection to that piece (the log described this as the selected piece moving from its old position to the clicked position, but this represented changing focus rather than physical motion across intervening cells). Movement actions (MOVE_LEFT, MOVE_RIGHT, MOVE_UP, MOVE_DOWN) attempted to move the currently selected piece one grid cell in the specified direction. If the adjacent cell in that direction was empty floor, the piece moved there. If it contained a pushable block and the cell beyond that was empty, the selected piece could push the block one cell while also moving into the block’s previous cell. If the adjacent cell was a wall, out of bounds, or a blocked configuration (e.g., a block that could not be pushed because something or a boundary was behind it), the move failed and the log reported that the selected piece did not change position. Walls and other solid obstacles never moved and always prevented passage or pushing through them. Steps were decremented by 1 for each MOVE_* or CLICK, so even selection changes consumed part of the limited step budget. The level ended either when the WIN condition was satisfied (typically by aligning a pushable object with a '+' goal tile) or when steps reached 0 without satisfying that condition, resulting in a lost run.

understanding.goal_understanding: The goal of the game was to achieve a WIN state on each level, typically by maneuvering a pushable object (or otherwise interactable piece) onto one or more goal tiles marked with '+'. Once a level reported WIN, that level was solved; once a run reported WIN, the entire game was considered won. Each level had a limited number of steps, so the objective also included planning an efficient sequence of moves and clicks before the step counter reached zero.
understanding.mechanics_understanding: The world consisted of a grid with different tiles: empty floor, walls/obstacles, selectable pieces, pushable blocks, and special goal tiles '+'. You interacted indirectly through a selected piece. CLICK [x, y] changed which piece was selected; if a piece existed at the clicked coordinates and was selectable, the game moved the selection to that piece (the log described this as the selected piece moving from its old position to the clicked position, but this represented changing focus rather than physical motion across intervening cells). Movement actions (MOVE_LEFT, MOVE_RIGHT, MOVE_UP, MOVE_DOWN) attempted to move the currently selected piece one grid cell in the specified direction. If the adjacent cell in that direction was empty floor, the piece moved there. If it contained a pushable block and the cell beyond that was empty, the selected piece could push the block one cell while also moving into the block’s previous cell. If the adjacent cell was a wall, out of bounds, or a blocked configuration (e.g., a block that could not be pushed because something or a boundary was behind it), the move failed and the log reported that the selected piece did not change position. Walls and other solid obstacles never moved and always prevented passage or pushing through them. Steps were decremented by 1 for each MOVE_* or CLICK, so even selection changes consumed part of the limited step budget. The level ended either when the WIN condition was satisfied (typically by aligning a pushable object with a '+' goal tile) or when steps reached 0 without satisfying that condition, resulting in a lost run.
